past_medical_history: Developmental delay, diagnosed in childhood — attended special education program through age 18
Epilepsy since childhood, poorly characterized seizure type, managed intermittently with phenytoin in home country
Right scalp soft tissue mass noted since birth, never evaluated with imaging
Alopecia patch over right parietal scalp, stable since childhood
family_history: Father deceased at age 55 from myocardial infarction
No known family history of epilepsy or seizure disorders
One younger sibling with no medical problems
Maternal uncle with possible thyroid disorder (details unknown)

social_history.occupation: Works part-time at a grocery store
social_history.living_situation: Recently arrived from overseas, staying with a relative in a shared apartment
social_history.exercise: Minimal structured exercise, walks for transportation

neurological_exam.mental_status: Patient is drowsy but arousable to voice. Oriented to person and place; uncertain about date and time. GCS 13 (E3V4M6) in the post-ictal period, improving. Speech is dysarthric with slowed rate but no paraphasic errors. Naming intact for high-frequency words, struggles with low-frequency items. Follows two-step commands after repetition. Recall is 1/3 at five minutes. Attention is impaired — serial 7s abandoned after two attempts.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Visual fields grossly full to confrontation though cooperation limited. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild left lower facial droop noted, unclear if longstanding. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk mildly decreased in left upper extremity compared to right. Tone normal throughout. Strength 4+/5 in left upper extremity grip and deltoid, 5/5 elsewhere bilaterally. No pronator drift observed when tested after post-ictal period improved. No involuntary movements noted at time of examination.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in all extremities. Proprioception intact in toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact at medial malleoli bilaterally. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar response flexor on left, equivocal on right.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric on the left, intact on the right. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements slow bilaterally, worse on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Deferred due to post-ictal state at presentation. Subsequently assessed: mildly unsteady, wide-based, but able to walk independently. Tandem gait impaired.
neurological_exam.additional: Right parietal scalp demonstrates a well-circumscribed patch of alopecia approximately 4 cm in diameter. Beneath the alopecia, a soft, non-tender, compressible subcutaneous mass is palpable, approximately 3 x 2 cm. Skin overlying the mass appears normal. Tongue laceration noted on right lateral border consistent with recent seizure.

patient.chief_complaint: Found unresponsive after prolonged generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Khatri is a 27-year-old right-handed man who was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his cousin found him on the floor of their apartment, unresponsive and with rhythmic jerking movements involving all four extremities. The cousin reports that he heard a loud thud from the patient's room and found him on the ground approximately two minutes later. The patient appeared to be having a generalized convulsion with tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs followed by rhythmic clonic jerking. The episode reportedly lasted approximately four to five minutes before gradually subsiding. The patient remained unresponsive for approximately fifteen minutes after the convulsive activity ceased. Emergency medical services arrived and administered intravenous lorazepam 2 mg. On arrival to the emergency department, the patient was obtunded with a GCS of 8, which gradually improved over approximately forty-five minutes to a GCS of 13.

Further history obtained from the cousin reveals that the patient has had epilepsy since childhood in his home country, where he was treated with phenytoin, though his compliance has been inconsistent since relocating three months ago due to difficulty obtaining refills. The cousin recalls that the patient has been having brief episodes approximately two to three times per week over the past two months during which he appears to zone out, stares blankly, and sometimes makes repetitive lip-smacking or hand-fumbling movements lasting twenty to forty seconds. The patient has limited recollection of these events but reports occasional episodes of a strange rising sensation in his stomach followed by a feeling of intense familiarity, as if he has experienced the current moment before. He has never had neuroimaging performed. The cousin also notes the longstanding alopecia patch and soft lump on the right side of the patient's scalp, which the family has always considered a birthmark. The patient sustained a minor head injury during the current fall, with a small abrasion over the right parietal region near the scalp mass, but there was no witnessed head strike. There has been no recent febrile illness, headache, or change in behavior aside from the increasing seizure frequency. The patient reports no recent alcohol or drug use.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha rhythm, mildly slow, reactive to eye opening, better developed on the left
background.continuity: Continuous with intermittent periods of attenuation over the right hemisphere
background.symmetry: Persistent asymmetry with lower amplitude and slower frequencies over the right hemisphere, particularly in the temporal and parieto-occipital regions
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves present; sleep spindles asymmetric, less well-formed over right hemisphere

findings.location: Right anterior to mid-temporal region (F8/T4), with occasional spread to right posterior temporal (T6)
findings.frequency: Intermittent, averaging 2-3 per 10-minute epoch, more frequent during drowsiness and light sleep
findings.morphology: Sharp waves with phase reversal at F8/T4, amplitude 80-120 microvolts, with consistent after-going slow wave component. Some are followed by brief runs of rhythmic theta at 5-6 Hz.
findings.state: Awake and drowsy states, activated during drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Morphology consistent with epileptiform discharges. Localization suggests right temporal epileptogenic focus.

findings.frequency: Intermittent polymorphic delta activity at 1-3 Hz, occurring in runs of 3-8 seconds
findings.morphology: Irregular polymorphic delta activity, moderate amplitude
findings.state: Present during wakefulness, more prominent during drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Suggests underlying structural or functional abnormality in the right temporal region.

findings.location: Diffuse right hemisphere, most prominent in temporal and parieto-occipital derivations
findings.morphology: Right hemisphere amplitudes approximately 60-70% of the left hemisphere
findings.clinical_correlation: May reflect underlying structural pathology involving the right hemisphere. Correlate with neuroimaging.

artifacts.description: Intermittent EMG artifact, more prominent on the right, partially obscuring temporal derivations in some epochs

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's post-ictal state and limited cooperation
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response. Photic driving present and symmetric.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG. Right anterior-to-mid temporal epileptiform discharges (sharp waves at F8/T4) with intermittent right temporal polymorphic delta slowing and right hemispheric amplitude asymmetry. Findings are consistent with a right temporal epileptogenic focus with possible underlying structural pathology. No electrographic seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation was not performed. Intermittent muscle artifact over temporal chains partially limits assessment of temporal sharp transients in some epochs. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is recommended for further characterization.
recommended_actions: Prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual seizures and further characterize ictal onset
Brain MRI with epilepsy protocol to evaluate for structural lesion given hemispheric asymmetry
Correlate with clinical semiology and neuroimaging findings

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Suppressed centrally with thin rim of perilesional gliosis showing FLAIR hyperintensity

findings.mass_effect: No mass effect; ex vacuo dilation of the adjacent right lateral ventricle trigone and occipital horn
findings.borders: Well-defined, lined by thin glial membrane, communicating with right lateral ventricle

findings.location: Right hemisphere diffusely, most pronounced in the parieto-occipital and temporal regions

signal_characteristics.T1: Sulcal widening and volume loss of the right cerebral hemisphere
signal_characteristics.T2: Compensatory ex vacuo enlargement of the right lateral ventricle

findings.location: Right parietal cortex adjacent to the porencephalic cyst, extending into right mesial temporal structures

signal_characteristics.T1: Hyperintense foci on non-contrast T1 consistent with calcification

findings.location: Right sylvian fissure, right ambient cistern, and right cerebellopontine angle cistern
findings.size: Largest focus 1.8 x 1.2 cm in the right sylvian fissure

signal_characteristics.Fat_suppression: Complete signal suppression confirming fat content

signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle increased T2 signal in the right hippocampal body
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Equivocal FLAIR hyperintensity — could represent early sclerosis or artifact from adjacent porencephalic cyst

findings.location: Right parietal bone, overlying the porencephalic cyst

signal_characteristics.T1: Thinning and inward remodeling of the right parietal calvarium

findings.location: Right parietal scalp, beneath the area of alopecia

volumetrics.right_hemisphere_volume: Reduced by approximately 15% compared to left

additional_observations: Left cerebral hemisphere appears structurally normal
No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage
Minimally depressed fracture line in the right parietal bone, likely acute given clinical history, without underlying epidural or subdural collection
Mild scattered periventricular white matter T2 hyperintensities of uncertain clinical significance
mri.impression: Complex right hemispheric abnormalities including a right parieto-occipital porencephalic cyst with communication to the right lateral ventricle, right cerebral hemiatrophy, multifocal intracranial lipomatous lesions (right sylvian fissure, ambient cistern, cerebellopontine angle cistern), cortical calcifications extending into right mesial temporal structures, right calvarial remodeling with overlying subcutaneous lipoma beneath an area of scalp alopecia, and right hippocampal volume loss with equivocal T2/FLAIR signal abnormality. Minimally depressed right parietal fracture without intracranial hemorrhage. Constellation of findings suggests a neurocutaneous syndrome. The right hippocampal changes and mesial temporal calcifications may represent the epileptogenic substrate.

differential_by_imaging.reasoning: Classic triad of cutaneous lipoma with alopecia (nevus psiloliparus), intracranial lipomas, and ipsilateral cerebral abnormalities

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Other neurocutaneous syndrome (Proteus, Delleman, epidermal nevus syndrome)
differential_by_imaging.reasoning: Some overlapping features but specific constellation most consistent with ECCL
Right hippocampal volume loss and signal change, though may be secondary to ipsilateral hemispheric pathology rather than primary MTS

recommended_actions: Epilepsy protocol MRI with thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes for further hippocampal characterization
Ophthalmologic examination to evaluate for choristomas (associated with ECCL)
Consider dermatology consultation for characterization of scalp lesion
Correlate with EEG findings for epilepsy localization

findings: Sinus tachycardia, likely related to post-ictal sympathetic activation
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with mild sinus tachycardia. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia or conduction abnormality detected.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Sinus tachycardia likely post-ictal in nature. No cardiac etiology for the patient's presentation suggested by this ECG.

Complete Blood Count.clinical_significance: Mild leukocytosis, may be stress-related or post-ictal

Basic Metabolic Panel.clinical_significance: Mild hyperglycemia, likely stress-related post-ictal catecholamine surge

Antiepileptic Drug Levels.clinical_significance: Significantly subtherapeutic — consistent with reported medication non-compliance
Subtherapeutic free level, confirming inadequate drug exposure

Additional Labs.clinical_significance: Elevated post-ictal prolactin, consistent with generalized tonic-clonic seizure (drawn within 30 minutes of event)
Elevated creatine kinase consistent with sustained muscular activity during generalized seizure
Elevated post-ictal lactate, expected after prolonged convulsive seizure
Mildly elevated; may be subclinical hypothyroidism or stress effect. Phenytoin can alter thyroid function tests.

labs.interpretation: Significant findings include markedly subtherapeutic phenytoin levels (total 4.2 mcg/mL, free 0.5 mcg/mL) confirming medication non-compliance as a contributing factor to seizure breakthrough. Post-ictal laboratory changes include mild leukocytosis, stress hyperglycemia, elevated prolactin, elevated CK, and elevated lactate — all consistent with a recent generalized tonic-clonic seizure. TSH is mildly elevated at 5.8 mIU/L, which may reflect subclinical hypothyroidism or phenytoin effect on thyroid hormone metabolism. Remaining metabolic panel and hepatic function are within normal limits.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8-9 Hz alpha rhythm, reactive, mildly asymmetric with lower amplitude over the right hemisphere
background.symmetry: Persistent right hemispheric amplitude attenuation and intermittent focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with asymmetric spindles

findings.location: Right anterior temporal (F8/T4) with consistent field extending to T6
findings.frequency: 12-18 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 30+ per hour during sleep
findings.morphology: Well-defined sharp waves and occasional spike-and-slow-wave complexes with clear phase reversal at F8/T4
findings.clinical_correlation: Highly consistent with right temporal epileptogenic focus

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right temporal chain
findings.frequency: Two clinical seizures captured over 72-hour monitoring period
findings.morphology: Ictal onset: rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta at F8/T4, evolving to 3-4 Hz rhythmic delta with spread to ipsilateral parasagittal and contralateral temporal regions over 45-65 seconds. Post-ictal right temporal suppression for 90 seconds.
findings.state: Both events occurred during wakefulness — one from waking state, one during drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Clinical correlate: behavioral arrest, oral automatisms (lip-smacking and chewing), left hand fumbling automatisms. Duration 55-65 seconds. Post-ictal confusion lasting 3-5 minutes with transient left-sided weakness. Semiology consistent with right mesial temporal onset.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Accentuated right temporal slowing; no electrographic seizure provoked
activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Increased frequency of interictal discharges during sleep

output.impression: Abnormal 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Two habitual clinical seizures captured, both with right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4) and characteristic temporal lobe semiology (behavioral arrest, oral and manual automatisms, post-ictal confusion). Frequent interictal right temporal epileptiform discharges. Findings confirm right temporal lobe epilepsy.
recommended_actions: Optimize antiepileptic therapy for focal epilepsy — consider transitioning from phenytoin to a newer agent such as levetiracetam or lacosamide
Evaluate for surgical candidacy given drug-resistant focal epilepsy with concordant EEG and imaging
Neuropsychological testing for lateralizing cognitive deficits

findings.location: Right hippocampus, body and tail segments
findings.size: Volume 2.0 mL (3 SD below age-matched mean)

signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Definite hyperintensity of right hippocampal body and tail on thin coronal cuts
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Confirmed FLAIR hyperintensity, most prominent in the hippocampal body
signal_characteristics.T1_coronal: Volume loss with loss of internal architecture and digitations

findings.mass_effect: None — volume loss rather than expansion
findings.borders: Diffuse involvement of hippocampal body and tail, head relatively spared

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle blurring of the gray-white matter junction in the right temporal pole, with mild FLAIR hyperintensity in temporal pole white matter

additional_observations: Previously described porencephalic cyst, intracranial lipomas, calcifications, and subcutaneous lipoma are confirmed and unchanged
output.impression: Epilepsy protocol MRI confirms right hippocampal sclerosis with significant volume loss, T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, and loss of internal architecture predominantly affecting the hippocampal body and tail. Associated right temporal pole blurring consistent with temporal pole signal abnormality seen in temporal lobe epilepsy. These findings, in the context of the complex right hemispheric abnormalities (porencephalic cyst, lipomas, calcifications), suggest a dual pathology etiology for this patient's right temporal lobe epilepsy.
recommended_actions: Multidisciplinary epilepsy surgery conference for surgical candidacy evaluation
FDG-PET for additional lateralizing and localizing information
Consider WADA test or fMRI for language lateralization prior to any surgical planning

Cognitive Assessment.clinical_significance: Borderline intellectual functioning, consistent with history of developmental delay
Significantly impaired, lateralizing to right hemisphere dysfunction
Impaired, consistent with right hemisphere pathology

Memory Testing.clinical_significance: Mild verbal memory deficit, may reflect bilateral temporal dysfunction or medication effect
Significantly impaired visual memory, lateralizing to right temporal dysfunction
Relatively preserved naming, suggesting left temporal function relatively intact

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile demonstrates borderline intellectual functioning (FSIQ 72) with a pattern of greater nonverbal than verbal deficits, consistent with right hemisphere dysfunction. Severely impaired visual memory with relatively preserved verbal memory and naming lateralizes to right temporal dysfunction, concordant with the right temporal lobe epilepsy focus. Overall cognitive profile is consistent with developmental delay superimposed on right hemisphere pathology.
abnormal_values_summary: Visual Memory: Severely impaired (lateralizing to right temporal)

findings.location: Right temporal lobe, most prominent in mesial temporal structures and temporal pole

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal region and right temporal pole, extending mildly into the right lateral temporal neocortex
Mildly reduced FDG uptake across the right hemisphere compared to the left, most pronounced in the parieto-occipital region corresponding to the known porencephalic cyst

additional_observations: Left hemisphere demonstrates normal symmetric FDG uptake
Cerebellum and brainstem show normal metabolism
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates marked right mesial temporal and temporal pole hypometabolism, concordant with right temporal lobe epilepsy focus identified by EEG and MRI. Additional mild right hemispheric hypometabolism corresponds to the structural abnormalities (porencephalic cyst, hemiatrophy). No evidence of contralateral temporal dysfunction. Findings strongly support right temporal seizure onset.
recommended_actions: Findings concordant with EEG and MRI — patient is a candidate for surgical evaluation
Consider right anterior temporal lobectomy or selective amygdalohippocampectomy
Pre-surgical WADA test recommended for memory lateralization

Repeat Antiepileptic Drug Levels (Day 3).clinical_significance: Now within therapeutic range after dose adjustment and medication compliance in hospital

output.interpretation: Repeat phenytoin levels after three days of supervised dosing show therapeutic total (12.8 mcg/mL) and free (1.4 mcg/mL) levels. This confirms that subtherapeutic levels on admission were due to medication non-compliance rather than altered pharmacokinetics. However, given the complex epilepsy syndrome and multiple structural lesions, seizure control with phenytoin monotherapy may be suboptimal even at therapeutic levels. Consider transitioning to a newer antiepileptic drug with a more favorable side-effect profile.

results.title: Encephalocraniocutaneous lipomatosis: a systematic review of the literature
results.summary: ECCL is a rare sporadic neurocutaneous syndrome characterized by unilateral skin, eye, and brain abnormalities. Epilepsy occurs in approximately 50-70% of cases. Seizures may be focal or generalized, and drug-resistant epilepsy is common. Surgical management including lesionectomy or temporal lobectomy may be considered in drug-resistant cases with a localizable seizure focus.

results.title: Epilepsy in neurocutaneous syndromes: diagnosis and management
results.summary: Reviews epilepsy management across neurocutaneous syndromes including ECCL. Drug-resistant focal epilepsy in ECCL may benefit from surgical evaluation when the epileptogenic zone is concordantly localized. Dual pathology (structural lesion plus hippocampal sclerosis) is well-described. Newer antiepileptic drugs (levetiracetam, lacosamide) are preferred over older agents for better tolerability.

results.title: Surgical outcomes in epilepsy associated with cortical malformations and neurocutaneous syndromes
results.summary: Favorable surgical outcomes (Engel class I) reported in 55-65% of patients with neurocutaneous syndrome-associated epilepsy when comprehensive pre-surgical evaluation demonstrates concordant localization. Dual pathology cases may have slightly lower seizure-freedom rates.

output.summary: Encephalocraniocutaneous lipomatosis (ECCL) is a rare neurocutaneous syndrome with epilepsy in 50-70% of cases, often drug-resistant. Management follows standard focal epilepsy guidelines with preference for newer AEDs. Epilepsy surgery is a viable option when pre-surgical evaluation shows concordant localization. Dual pathology (structural lesion plus hippocampal sclerosis) is recognized. Surgical outcomes are favorable in 55-65% of well-selected cases.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg BID as add-on or replacement for phenytoin
interactions: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction between levetiracetam and phenytoin — levetiracetam is not hepatically metabolized and does not induce or inhibit CYP enzymes
warnings: If replacing phenytoin, taper phenytoin gradually to avoid withdrawal seizures
Levetiracetam may cause behavioral side effects (irritability, mood changes) — monitor given patient's developmental delay
Dose adjustment may be needed for renal function; current creatinine is normal
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization needed
alternatives: Lacosamide 50 mg BID titrated to 100-200 mg BID — well-tolerated, fewer behavioral side effects
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg BID titrated to 600-1200 mg BID — effective for focal epilepsy but monitor sodium levels
Brivaracetam 25 mg BID — similar mechanism to levetiracetam with potentially fewer behavioral effects

Thyroid Function.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, persistent on repeat testing
Low-normal free T4; phenytoin increases T4 metabolism via hepatic enzyme induction
Negative, no evidence of autoimmune thyroiditis

output.interpretation: Mildly elevated TSH with low-normal free T4 is most consistent with phenytoin effect on thyroid hormone metabolism (phenytoin induces hepatic CYP enzymes, increasing T4 clearance). Anti-TPO antibodies are negative, arguing against primary autoimmune thyroiditis. If phenytoin is discontinued, TSH should be rechecked in 6-8 weeks. No thyroid treatment indicated at this time.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy — temporal lobe (right temporal lobe epilepsy secondary to encephalocraniocutaneous lipomatosis with dual pathology including hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.diagnosis: Focal epilepsy secondary to porencephalic cyst alone
differential.key_distinguishing: Porencephalic cyst is parieto-occipital, but EEG and semiology localize to temporal lobe, suggesting the mesial temporal/hippocampal pathology is the primary epileptogenic substrate

differential.diagnosis: Focal epilepsy secondary to cortical calcifications
differential.key_distinguishing: Calcifications extend into mesial temporal structures and may contribute to epileptogenicity, but hippocampal sclerosis on MRI and temporal EEG focus point to mesial temporal onset
Elevated post-ictal prolactin, tongue laceration, EEG correlate, and characteristic temporal lobe semiology argue strongly against PNES

differential.diagnosis: Structural epilepsy secondary to brain tumor (low-grade glioma)
differential.key_distinguishing: MRI findings show non-enhancing lipomatous lesions with fat signal (confirming lipomas, not tumor), porencephalic cyst rather than neoplasm, and no enhancing or expansile mass lesion

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity and localize seizure focus
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right temporal epileptiform discharges with hemispheric asymmetry

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural cause of epilepsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complex right hemispheric abnormalities including porencephalic cyst, lipomas, calcifications, hippocampal asymmetry, and subcutaneous lipoma with overlying alopecia

optimal_actions.action: Interpret laboratory results including antiepileptic drug levels
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtherapeutic phenytoin levels confirming non-compliance; post-ictal lab changes; mildly elevated TSH

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to rule out cardiac etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with post-ictal sinus tachycardia, no arrhythmia

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual seizures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Captured right temporal seizures with characteristic semiology

optimal_actions.action: Request epilepsy protocol MRI for detailed hippocampal evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed right hippocampal sclerosis with dual pathology

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on ECCL and epilepsy management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting surgical evaluation for drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy in neurocutaneous syndromes

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating new AED
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam has no significant interaction with phenytoin

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to confirm epilepsy and localize seizure focus
Obtain brain MRI to identify structural etiology
Check antiepileptic drug levels given history of non-compliance
Recognize the constellation of findings (scalp lipoma with alopecia, intracranial lipomas, porencephalic cyst, hemiatrophy) as a neurocutaneous syndrome (ECCL)
Ensure medication compliance and optimize antiepileptic therapy
Identify right hippocampal sclerosis as the likely primary epileptogenic substrate
contraindicated_actions: Discharging patient without neuroimaging given first-time presentation with complex seizure semiology and abnormal exam
Attributing seizures solely to medication non-compliance without investigating structural etiology
Performing lumbar puncture without first ruling out mass effect or elevated intracranial pressure given the complex intracranial pathology
Initiating carbamazepine without checking for HLA-B*1502 (given South Asian ethnicity — risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome)
Abruptly discontinuing phenytoin without bridging to an alternative AED
key_reasoning_points: The combination of scalp alopecia with subcutaneous lipoma (nevus psiloliparus), intracranial lipomas, porencephalic cyst, hemicerebral atrophy, and cortical calcifications is characteristic of encephalocraniocutaneous lipomatosis (ECCL)

While the porencephalic cyst is parieto-occipital, the seizure semiology (auras with epigastric rising and déjà vu, behavioral arrest, oral automatisms) and EEG localization point to a right temporal lobe onset
Dual pathology — ECCL-related structural abnormalities plus right hippocampal sclerosis — is the most complete explanation for this patient's drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy
Subtherapeutic phenytoin levels from non-compliance are a major reversible factor contributing to seizure breakthrough, but may not be the sole cause given the complex structural pathology
Mildly elevated TSH is likely a phenytoin effect on thyroid hormone metabolism rather than a primary thyroid disorder — a potential red herring that should not distract from the epilepsy workup

The differential must distinguish ECCL from other neurocutaneous syndromes: Proteus syndrome (progressive asymmetric overgrowth), Delleman-Oorthuys syndrome (supra-auricular hypoplastic skin defect), oculoectodermal syndrome (no intracranial lipomas), and epidermal nevus syndromes (different cutaneous manifestations)

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: The case presents with a breakthrough generalized seizure in a patient with known epilepsy and medication non-compliance, which could lead to an incomplete workup. However, the complex MRI findings (porencephalic cyst, intracranial lipomas, calcifications) combined with a scalp lipoma beneath alopecia form a pattern that the agent must recognize as a neurocutaneous syndrome (ECCL). The hippocampal pathology is subtle on initial MRI and requires epilepsy protocol imaging to confirm. The mildly elevated TSH serves as a red herring that could distract from the core epilepsy evaluation.
